sourceFiles.name: vehicle-model

fix.title: Raise exemplar engine power to satisfy performance requirement
fix.description: This fix makes the representative instance `myCar` physically plausible with respect to the stated performance requirement by increasing `myCar.engine.power` from 250.0 to 300.0. The example vehicle no longer contradicts the requirement `vehicle.engine.power >= 300.0`, so reviewers will not misinterpret the exemplar as a satisfying configuration that is actually non-compliant.

changes.description: Updated the exemplar vehicle instance so its engine power satisfies the stated minimum performance requirement, resolving the plausibility issue without introducing a separate non-compliant variant.

fix.title: Replace surrogate braking check with operational braking subsystem and stopping-distance requirement
fix.description: This fix resolves the plausibility defect by replacing the non-physical mass-per-wheel surrogate with an operationally meaningful braking model. The updated model introduces a BrakeSubsystem with brake torque and thermal capacity, tire-road friction on each wheel, and a SafetyReq that constrains stopping distance from a defined initial speed while also checking available brake force against required deceleration and ensuring thermal capacity is positive. This makes the requirement traceable to recognizable braking performance factors instead of an invalid proxy.

changes.description: Replaced the implausible braking surrogate in SafetyReq with an operational braking requirement based on defined speed, stopping distance, brake torque, tire-road friction, and thermal capacity.

Added an explicit BrakeSubsystem to model brake torque capacity, effective braking radius, thermal capacity, and maximum achievable deceleration, which are necessary operational artifacts for braking adequacy.
Updated Vehicle to include the braking subsystem so the safety requirement has a concrete subject structure to constrain.
Extended Wheel with tire-road friction so braking performance is tied to available tire grip rather than wheel count alone.
Populated the example vehicle with brake subsystem values and wheel/tire friction attributes so the updated model is complete and directly usable.

fix.title: Replace electrical propulsion interface with mechanical torque-speed interface
fix.description: This fix makes the propulsion architecture explicitly conventional and physically plausible by replacing the voltage/current-based PowerPort with a mechanical TorqueSpeedPort used between the engine, transmission, and vehicle driveline. It also aligns the engine's power attribute with the port semantics through a mechanical power consistency constraint and updates the example instance to provide torque and shaft speed values consistent with the engine power. This directly resolves the architectural implausibility of modeling an internal-combustion drivetrain's main propulsion transfer as an electrical interface.

changes.description: Replaced the implausible electrical propulsion interface with a mechanical torque-speed port on Vehicle and Engine, and added corresponding transmission shaft ports to reflect a conventional automotive drivetrain.

Added an explicit requirement stating that the conventional vehicle propulsion interface shall be mechanical torque-speed, making the intended propulsion architecture explicit as recommended by the plausibility criterion.
Updated the example engine instance to populate the new mechanical shaft port with values consistent with the engine power attribute, preserving analyzability after the interface change.

fix.title: Define electrical units and raise PowerPort limit to a plausible traction-power level
fix.description: This fix makes the PowerPort semantics explicit by adding unit-bearing attributes for voltage, current, and power, and by constraining transferred power in watts through a named maxPower attribute. The previous unqualified `voltage * current <= 1000.0` limit is replaced with a 300000 W cap, which is consistent with the existing vehicle performance requirement (`engine.power >= 300.0`) when interpreted as kW and with the example vehicle architecture. The example instance is updated to provide concrete port values that satisfy the revised constraint, eliminating the ambiguity and implausibility identified in the finding.

changes.description: Replaced the ambiguous PowerPort attributes and implausible 1000.0 cap with explicitly unit-annotated electrical quantities and a plausible maximum transferred power in watts.

results.name: Contextual Plausibility
results.rawText:
Model Characterisation
- System purpose: A basic passenger road vehicle model intended to represent a car with engine, transmission, wheels, driver, and high-level performance/safety requirements.
- Operational domain: Automotive / road transport.
- Lifecycle phase: Early concept to preliminary design. The model contains high-level parts, attributes, simple constraints, and requirements, but lacks detailed physical architecture, allocations, and verification detail.
- Model scope: Partial system model / illustrative fragment rather than a complete vehicle definition. It shows a structural view with a few requirements and one example instance (`myCar`).
- Known scope limitations: The model does not include braking subsystem design, fuel/energy source, thermal management, suspension, steering, communications, control software, environment definition, maintenance concept, or detailed timing behavior. Checks depending on those omitted aspects are noted as out of scope where appropriate.

ISSUE [PLAUSIBILITY-001]
Element:        VehicleModel::PerformanceReq
Check:          1. Physical Plausibility
Finding:        The performance requirement claims 0-60 mph in less than 6 seconds, but the requirement constraint only checks `vehicle.engine.power >= 300.0` with no unit definition and no relation to vehicle mass, drivetrain losses, traction, aerodynamic drag, or wheel torque. As written, the model implies that engine power alone is sufficient to establish the acceleration target, which is not physically credible.
Evidence:       `doc /* The vehicle shall accelerate from 0 to 60 mph in less than 6 seconds */` and `require constraint { vehicle.engine.power >= 300.0 }`
Domain Basis:   Vehicle acceleration performance depends on power-to-weight ratio, traction limits, gearing, drivetrain efficiency, and resistive forces; engine power alone is not a sufficient physical predictor of 0-60 time.
Score:          0.55 - Moderate plausibility gap because the model risks supporting an incorrect feasibility judgement for performance.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         The model may falsely indicate that the design satisfies acceleration performance when a real vehicle with the same nominal power may not.
Recommendation: Replace the proxy requirement with a physically grounded performance model including units, vehicle mass, drivetrain efficiency, wheel radius, traction assumptions, and a derived or simulated acceleration metric.

ISSUE [PLAUSIBILITY-002]
Element:        VehicleModel::myCar
Check:          1. Physical Plausibility
Finding:        The example instance appears inconsistent with the stated acceleration requirement because `myCar` has engine power 250.0 while the performance requirement requires at least 300.0. This undermines plausibility of the exemplar vehicle as a satisfying configuration.
Evidence:       `part myCar : Vehicle { :>> engine { :>> power = 250.0; ... } }` and `require constraint { vehicle.engine.power >= 300.0 }`
Domain Basis:   A representative design instance should not contradict a stated key performance requirement unless explicitly identified as non-compliant, exploratory, or a different variant.
Score:          0.60 - Moderate issue because it creates a plausible non-compliant example in a small model where the single instance is likely to be interpreted as representative.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Reviewers may incorrectly assume the illustrated vehicle is feasible against its own performance requirement.
Recommendation: Either raise `myCar.engine.power` to a justified value, mark the instance as intentionally non-compliant, or define separate compliant/non-compliant variants.

CHECK PASSED: 2. Timing & Sequencing Plausibility  -  No explicit timing, latency, watchdog, startup, human reaction, or causal event sequencing values are modelled, so no direct timing-plausibility defect can be substantiated from the text.

CHECK PASSED: 3. Environmental Plausibility  -  No operating environment, temperature, vibration, EMC, humidity, altitude, or lifetime exposure assumptions are specified, so there is no explicit environmentally implausible claim to assess in this fragment.

ISSUE [PLAUSIBILITY-003]
Element:        VehicleModel::SafetyReq
Check:          4. Operational Plausibility
Finding:        The safety requirement states the vehicle shall have adequate braking performance, but the actual constraint uses only `vehicle.mass / vehicle.wheels.size <= 500.0`, with no brake subsystem, tire-road friction, brake torque, thermal capacity, stopping distance, deceleration target, or speed condition. This is not an operationally plausible representation of braking adequacy.
Evidence:       `doc /* The vehicle shall have adequate braking performance */` and `require constraint { vehicle.mass / vehicle.wheels.size <= 500.0 }`
Domain Basis:   Braking performance is governed by brake force/torque, tire grip, vehicle speed, load transfer, ABS/control behavior, and thermal fade; mass divided by wheel count is not an engineering-valid operational measure of braking adequacy.
Score:          0.45 - Significant defect because it can support an invalid safety conclusion about braking capability.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         A design could be judged safe without any evidence that it can stop within required distance or sustain repeated braking loads.
Recommendation: Model a braking subsystem and replace the surrogate constraint with operational measures such as stopping distance from defined speeds, deceleration limits, brake torque capacity, tire-road friction assumptions, and thermal fade margins.

CHECK PASSED: 5. External System & Environment Assumptions  -  The model does not assume GPS, network services, infrastructure behavior, external link budgets, or external-user behavior, so no explicit external-assumption plausibility issue is evidenced.

ISSUE [PLAUSIBILITY-004]
Element:        VehicleModel::PowerPort, VehicleModel::Vehicle, VehicleModel::Engine
Check:          6. Architectural Plausibility
Finding:        The vehicle and engine exchange power through `PowerPort` defined only by `voltage` and `current` with a constraint `voltage * current <= 1000.0`, while the engine has a separate `power` attribute and the example engine power is 250.0. For a conventional automotive mechanical drivetrain, representing the main propulsion power interface as an electrical voltage/current port is architecturally implausible unless this is explicitly an EV or hybrid electrical interface, which the model does not state.
Evidence:       `port def PowerPort { attribute voltage : Real; attribute current : Real; constraint powerLimit { voltage * current <= 1000.0 } }`, `part def Vehicle { ... port powerOut : PowerPort; }`, and `part def Engine { attribute power : Real; ... port powerPort : PowerPort; }`
Domain Basis:   Internal-combustion engine to transmission power transfer is mechanical torque/speed, not primarily an electrical voltage/current interface. Architectural interface types should match the physical integration path.
Score:          0.40 - Significant defect because the interface abstraction is likely to misrepresent how the subsystems can actually integrate.
Confidence:     MEDIUM - The issue depends on interpreting the vehicle as a conventional car; if the author intended an electric propulsion architecture, the model does not say so and still lacks consistency between electrical and mechanical power semantics.
Impact:         Architecture decisions, interface analyses, and feasibility reasoning may proceed on an unrealistic integration concept.
Recommendation: Define propulsion architecture explicitly. If conventional, replace with mechanical power/torque-speed ports. If electric, model battery/inverter/motor chain and relate electrical and mechanical power consistently with efficiencies and units.

ISSUE [PLAUSIBILITY-005]
Element:        VehicleModel::PowerPort
Check:          6. Architectural Plausibility
Finding:        The `PowerPort` constraint caps transferred power at `voltage * current <= 1000.0`, but no units are defined and this cap is potentially incompatible with the modelled propulsion performance. If interpreted as watts, 1000 W is far below any plausible road-vehicle propulsion level and far below the engine power values used elsewhere.
Evidence:       `constraint powerLimit { voltage * current <= 1000.0 }` and `part myCar : Vehicle { :>> engine { :>> power = 250.0; ... } }`
Domain Basis:   Road vehicles typically require propulsion power orders of magnitude above 1 kW for normal highway-capable performance. Power-interface limits must be consistent with system-level propulsion demands and units.
Score:          0.35 - Significant defect because, under the natural interpretation of voltage-current product as electrical power, the architecture becomes implausible for the stated vehicle capability.
Confidence:     MEDIUM - Certainty is limited because the model omits units; if `power` is in kW and port product is in some other unit basis, the conflict could be partially artefactual.
Impact:         The model can imply an impossible or grossly underpowered energy transfer interface relative to claimed vehicle performance.
Recommendation: Add units to all physical quantities, define the semantics of `PowerPort`, and ensure interface power limits are consistent with propulsion power requirements and the selected vehicle architecture.

CHECK PASSED: 7. Causal Plausibility  -  The model contains no explicit control loops, feedback paths, or dynamic information-flow sequences that would demonstrate a causal impossibility.

Summary Table

| ID | Score | Confidence | One-line description |
|---|---:|---|---|
| PLAUSIBILITY-001 | 0.55 | HIGH | Acceleration requirement uses engine power alone as a physically insufficient proxy for 0-60 performance. |
| PLAUSIBILITY-002 | 0.60 | HIGH | Example vehicle instance appears non-compliant with the stated performance requirement. |
| PLAUSIBILITY-003 | 0.45 | HIGH | Braking adequacy is represented by an implausible mass-per-wheel surrogate rather than braking physics. |
| PLAUSIBILITY-004 | 0.40 | MEDIUM | Propulsion integration is modelled with an electrical voltage/current port that is architecturally implausible for an unstated conventional vehicle. |
| PLAUSIBILITY-005 | 0.35 | MEDIUM | PowerPort transfer limit is potentially far below plausible vehicle propulsion needs and conflicts with engine power semantics. |

Overall Assessment Score: 0.47

Model Status for this Dimension: Critical deficiencies - major rework required before this model can be used.
